title: Confirming Your Calling and Election
subtitle: Growing in virtue, remembering apostolic eyewitness testimony, heeding prophetic Scripture
prayer_prompt: As Peter urges you to add to your faith virtue, knowledge, self-control, and love, consider asking God which link in that chain is weakest in you — and what one step would strengthen it.

heb.paragraph: A faith of equal standing (isotimos) with ours - the recipients share the same valuable faith as the apostles. No second-class Christians.
Knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord - epignosis is intensive knowledge, relational and transformative, not merely informational.
Partakers of the divine nature - a striking phrase. Believers share in God's nature, escaping the corruption of the world.

refs.note: Our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.
All the promises of God find their Yes in him.
Conformed to the image of his Son.
Christ in you, the hope of glory.

notes.note: Simeon Peter, servant and apostle - the Hebrew form of his name. Faith of equal standing emphasizes equality among believers.
Grace and peace multiplied through knowledge. Divine power has granted all things for life and godliness through knowing him.
Precious and very great promises make us partakers of the divine nature, having escaped worldly corruption through lust.

Calvin notes that partaking of divine nature does not mean becoming God but sharing in God's moral attributes through regeneration. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase our God and Savior Jesus Christ is one of the clearest NT affirmations of Christ's deity. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Divine nature (theias phuseos) is unique in the NT. Peter uses Hellenistic language to describe transformation into God's likeness. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the high Christology: Jesus is explicitly called God and Savior. Knowledge of Christ is the source of spiritual life. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the Hellenistic vocabulary: divine nature, escape corruption. Peter uses cultural language to express Christian truth. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.historical: Second Peter addresses the threat of false teachers who deny Christ's return (parousia) and promote moral license. The author presents himself as the apostle Peter writing shortly before his death (1:14), though many scholars date the letter later due to its developed theology, reference to Paul's letters as 'Scripture' (3:16), and literary dependence on Jude. The false teachers mock the delay of Christ's return ('Where is the promise of his coming?' 3:4) and use this skepticism to justify immoral living. The letter defends apostolic eyewitness testimony (1:16-18), warns of judgment using Old Testament examples (2:4-10), and explains that God's patience allows time for repentance (3:9).
hist.context: Peter, servant and apostle, writes to those who have obtained a faith of equal value through the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ. Grace and peace are multiplied through knowledge. His divine power has granted everything for life and godliness through knowledge of him who called us by his glory and excellence. Through these he has granted precious promises, that believers may become partakers of the divine nature, escaping worldly corruption.

heb.paragraph: Add to your faith virtue - arete is moral excellence, the excellence that makes something what it should be.
Make every effort (spoude) to supplement your faith - diligence is required. Grace does not eliminate human responsibility.
Make your calling and election sure (bebaios) - not that election is uncertain but that assurance comes through diligent pursuit of virtue.

refs.note: The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control.
Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling.
Show the same earnestness to have the full assurance of hope.
Faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.

notes.note: Make every effort to add to faith: virtue, knowledge, self-control, steadfastness, godliness, brotherly affection, love.
If these qualities are yours and increasing, you will not be ineffective or unfruitful. Whoever lacks them is blind, having forgotten past cleansing.
Be diligent to confirm your calling and election. Practice these things and you will never fall. Entry into the eternal kingdom will be richly provided.

Calvin notes the logical progression: each virtue builds on the previous. Faith is the foundation; love is the capstone. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb supplement (epichoregeo) means to supply generously, as a patron would fund a production. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Blindness and nearsightedness describe spiritual dullness. Such a person has forgotten the significance of conversion. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the ladder of virtues is not legalism but the natural fruit of genuine faith. Assurance is tied to sanctification. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that the virtue list resembles Stoic catalogs but is reoriented around faith and love. Greek form serves Christian content. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: For this reason, believers must make every effort to supplement faith with virtue, knowledge, self-control, steadfastness, godliness, brotherly affection, and love. If these qualities are present and increasing, they prevent ineffectiveness and unfruitfulness in knowing Christ. Whoever lacks them is blind, having forgotten cleansing from past sins. Therefore be diligent to confirm calling and election, for those who practice these things will never fall. Entry into the eternal kingdom will be richly provided.

heb.paragraph: After my departure (exodos) - Peter anticipates his death as an exodus, echoing Moses and the conversation at the Transfiguration.
We were eyewitnesses (epoptai) of his majesty - Peter appeals to firsthand observation of the Transfiguration as proof.
Until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts - the second coming brings full illumination.

refs.note: All Scripture is breathed out by God.
Moses and Elijah spoke of his departure (exodus).

notes.note: Peter will remind them repeatedly. His departure is imminent. He wants them to recall these things after he is gone.
We did not follow myths but were eyewitnesses of his majesty. We heard the voice from heaven on the holy mountain.
The prophetic word is more fully confirmed. No prophecy is a matter of private interpretation. Men spoke from God as the Spirit carried them.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Exodus (departure) was used at the Transfiguration (Luke 9:31) for Jesus's death. Peter's death is also an exodus.
More fully confirmed - the prophetic word is confirmed by the Transfiguration. Fulfilled prophecy validates Scripture.
Carried along (pheromenoi) by the Holy Spirit - the same verb for a ship driven by wind. Prophets were moved by divine power.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that Peter grounds authority in eyewitness experience (Transfiguration) and Spirit-inspired Scripture. Both testify to Christ. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids emphasizes that Peter's testimony is not myth (mythos) but history (epoptai). The Transfiguration was real. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Peter intends to remind them of these things repeatedly, knowing that his departure is imminent as Christ revealed. He wants them to recall these truths after his death. The apostles did not follow cleverly devised myths but were eyewitnesses of Christ's majesty at the Transfiguration. They heard the voice from heaven: This is my beloved Son. They have the prophetic word more fully confirmed. Prophecy did not come by human will but by the Holy Spirit speaking through men.

lit.note: Chapter 1 establishes foundations: divine provision, moral growth, and apostolic-prophetic authority.

themes.note: Holiness dominates through the virtue ladder. Prophecy is strong in the eyewitness and Scripture section. Faith is foundational.

discourse.title: 2 Peter 1 — Calling, Election, and the Apostolic Witness
discourse.thesis: Peter writes to confirm his readers' calling and election: God's divine power has given them everything for life and godliness; they should add to their faith successive virtues to confirm their calling.

nodes.label: Greeting and divine provision (1:1-4)
nodes.text: Simeon Peter, slave and apostle of Jesus Christ, to those who have obtained faith of equal standing. May grace and peace be multiplied. His divine power has granted everything pertaining to life and godliness, through the knowledge of him who called us. Through these he granted precious and very great promises, that you may become partakers of the divine nature, escaping corruption in the world.

nodes.label: The chain of virtues (1:5-11)
nodes.text: For this very reason, make every effort to add to your faith virtue, knowledge, self-control, perseverance, godliness, brotherly affection, love. If these qualities are increasing, they keep you from being ineffective and unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord. The one lacking them is short-sighted, having forgotten cleansing from past sins. Be all the more diligent to confirm your calling and election; doing this you will never fall, and entry into the eternal kingdom will be richly provided.

nodes.label: The reliability of apostolic witness and prophetic word (1:12-21)
nodes.text: Peter writes to remind even the established believers, knowing his death is near (Christ has shown him this). The apostolic message is not cleverly devised myth — Peter was an eyewitness of Christ's majesty at the Transfiguration, hearing the voice 'this is my beloved Son.' Yet the prophetic word is more fully confirmed; pay attention to it as a lamp shining in a dark place until the day dawns. No prophecy of Scripture comes from human will; men spoke from God as carried along by the Holy Spirit.

discourse.note: v.20-21's 'no prophecy of Scripture is a matter of one's own interpretation... men spoke from God as carried along by the Holy Spirit' is a cornerstone NT text on Scripture's inspiration, paralleling 2 Tim 3:16 from the apostolic-witness side.

hebtext.note: 2 Peter's signature term, used four times in chapter 1 (vv.2, 3, 5, 8). Epignōsis is intensified gnōsis — full, recognising knowledge rather than mere acquaintance. The repeated use marks the letter's anti-false-teaching framework: genuine epignōsis of Christ produces life-and-godliness; the false teachers' counterfeit knowledge produces moral degradation.

v.4's iconic phrase, foundational to Eastern Orthodox theology of theōsis (deification). The phrase does not mean ontological-equality with God but transformative-participation in the divine life. Western theology tends to handle this through sanctification language; Eastern tradition develops it as the heart of salvation. The phrase has driven major Christological-soteriological dialogue between traditions.

v.10's verb bebaioō (confirm/make firm) governs the chain-of-virtues argument. The growing virtues do not earn or create calling but provide confirmation-evidence of it. The Reformed tradition reads this as 'evidence of, not basis of' assurance. The chapter's pastoral aim is helping readers see the reality of their calling through fruit-evidence.

ppl.name: Simeon Peter (the apostle)
ppl.text: The opening uses 'Simeon Peter' (Symeōn Petros) — the Hebraised form occurring elsewhere only in Acts 15:14. The double-name links the apostle's pre-and-post-Christian identity. Peter writes near death (1:14), making 2 Peter functionally his testamentary letter — what he most needs his readers to remember as he prepares to die.

ppl.name: The eyewitness apostolic group
ppl.text: Peter's reference to being 'eyewitness of his majesty' on the holy mountain identifies the Transfiguration scene (Matt 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36). 'We' includes James and John who also heard the voice. Peter cites the Transfiguration not the Resurrection — perhaps because the false-teachers were denying Christ's coming-glory, which the Transfiguration uniquely previewed.

ppl.name: The prophetic-Scripture writers
ppl.text: Peter affirms the prophets as 'men who spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.' The verb pheromenoi (carried along) is nautical — sailors borne by wind. The metaphor preserves both human authorship (the men spoke) and divine-Spirit-driven inspiration (carried by the Spirit). The verses anchor Christian doctrine of biblical inspiration.

ppl.name: Christ at the Transfiguration
ppl.text: Christ in his transfigured glory is the chapter's Christological centre. Peter's appeal to Transfiguration-eyewitness establishes the apostolic message's grounding in observed reality, not invented myth. The Father's voice ('this is my beloved Son') is Peter's evidence that Christ's coming-kingdom (which the false teachers deny) has already been previewed in apostolic experience.

rec.text: Eusebius classifies 2 Peter among the 'disputed' (antilegomena) writings — known but its authenticity questioned. Eusebius himself does not deny Petrine authorship but reports the church's mixed reception. The letter's stylistic differences from 1 Peter (likely written through Silvanus's secretarial hand, 1 Pet 5:12) are noted as the basis for ancient doubt.

Calvin defended Petrine authorship while acknowledging stylistic differences from 1 Peter. He argued the testamentary character (1:14) and content's apostolic-authority justified the church's eventual canonization. His exposition of the virtues-chain (vv.5-7) became influential in Reformed pastoral theology of progressive sanctification.

Bauckham's landmark commentary defended 2 Peter's coherence, theological substance, and likely Petrine derivation. He treated the letter as testamentary genre with Petrine origin, explaining stylistic differences through genre and possibly different secretarial hands. His scholarly defense has shifted the consensus toward acceptance of the letter's apostolic standing.

rec.who: Eastern Orthodox tradition on theosis (2 Pet 1:4)
rec.text: The Greek Orthodox tradition (Athanasius, Maximus the Confessor, Palamas) developed theosis (deification) doctrine substantially from 2 Pet 1:4's 'partakers of the divine nature.' The verse is foundational to Eastern soteriology, which sees salvation as transformative union with divine life rather than purely juridical-acquittal. The Orthodox treatment of this verse has driven major ecumenical dialogue with Western traditions.

thread.text: 'Partakers of the divine nature' parallels Jesus's high-priestly prayer for believers' unity-with-the-divine ('that they may be one as we are one') and Paul's 'attaining... the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ.' The transformative-union theme is consistent across Johannine, Pauline, and Petrine writings.

Peter's chain-of-virtues parallels Paul's fruit-of-the-Spirit list (Gal 5) and the chain of trial→endurance→character→hope (Rom 5). The chain-form of progressive Christian formation is shared across NT writers — sanctification as cumulative-virtue-building rather than single-event achievement.

Peter's Transfiguration-eyewitness reference confirms the Synoptic-Gospels' Transfiguration narratives. 2 Peter is the only NT epistle explicitly grounding doctrine in Transfiguration witness; the cross-reference establishes apostolic-eyewitness as theological-foundation for understanding Christ's coming-glory.

Peter's Scripture-inspiration formulation pairs with Paul's 'all Scripture is breathed out by God' (2 Tim 3:16) and Hebrews' 'God spoke to our fathers by the prophets in many parts and many ways.' The three NT texts together establish the canonical doctrine of biblical inspiration through prophetic-apostolic mediation.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHis divine power has given us everything we need for a godly life through our knowledge of him who called us by his own glory and goodness.KJVAccording as his divine power hath given unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness, through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue:NIVThrough these he has given us his very great and precious promises, so that through them you may participate in the divine nature, having escaped the corruption in the world caused by evil desires.KJVWhereby are given unto us exceeding great and precious promises: that by these ye might be partakers of the divine nature, having escaped the corruption that is in the world through lust.NIVFor this very reason, make every effort to add to your faith goodness; and to goodness, knowledge;KJVAnd beside this, giving all diligence, add to your faith virtue; and to virtue knowledge;NIVTherefore, my brothers and sisters, make every effort to confirm your calling and election. For if you do these things, you will never stumble,KJVWherefore the rather, brethren, give diligence to make your calling and election sure: for if ye do these things, ye shall never fall:NIVFor we did not follow cleverly devised stories when we told you about the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ in power, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.KJVFor we have not followed cunningly devised fables, when we made known unto you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but were eyewitnesses of his majesty.NIVAbove all, you must understand that no prophecy of Scripture came about by the prophet’s own interpretation of things.KJVKnowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation.NIVFor prophecy never had its origin in the human will, but prophets, though human, spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.KJVFor the prophecy came not in old time by the will of man: but holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost.

coaching.tip: 'His divine power has granted to us all things that pertain to life and godliness' (v. 3). Notice: 'has granted' — past tense. We're not waiting for resources; we're using what's given. Peter's list of virtues to add (vv. 5-7) assumes the foundation is already laid. Christian growth isn't acquiring but deploying.

The ladder of virtues (faith → virtue → knowledge → self-control → steadfastness → godliness → brotherly affection → love) has a logic: each builds on the previous. You can't skip steps — self-control requires knowledge, godliness requires steadfastness. The sequence implies a curriculum, not a checklist. Spiritual growth has order.

Peter insists he's not following 'cleverly devised myths' but 'was an eyewitness of his majesty' (v. 16). The reference is the transfiguration: 'We heard this voice... when we were with him on the holy mountain' (v. 18). Apostolic testimony grounds faith in historical event. The gospel isn't story but report.
